floyd-county:
Floyd County is Elite Catastrophe's home ground, and we know its trees the way you only can when you live under them. Rome sits where the Oostanaula and the Etowah join to form the Coosa, and those river valleys, along with the hills that rise around them, set the pattern for how trees grow here and how they fail. The historic neighborhoods near downtown carry a canopy of water oaks and red oaks that were tall before most of the houses under them were built, their limbs reaching over rooflines, driveways, and power lines. Out in Cave Spring and the old mill streets of Lindale, the lots sit closer together, and a single mature hardwood can shade half a property and threaten two houses at once.

The tree trouble in Floyd County usually comes from a few familiar directions. Water oaks decline from the inside, so a trunk that looks solid from the sidewalk can be hollow at the base. Tall loblolly pines, common on the newer edges of the county, stand shallow-rooted in red clay that turns soft after a long rain, and a wet spring is all it takes to loosen the grip that holds them upright. Sweetgums shed heavy limbs in summer wind, and any tree leaning over a bedroom is a problem best handled on a calm day rather than during the next storm.

Our everyday work across Floyd County is steady and straightforward: careful removals in tight river-district yards, structural pruning to lift limbs off roofs and lines, crown cleaning for the older oaks worth keeping, and stump grinding so a finished job leaves you a usable yard. When severe weather crosses the county, Elite Catastrophe answers storm calls around the clock. Most days, though, the work is quieter than that, and we would rather look at a questionable tree while it is still standing than after it has picked its own moment to come down.
polk-county:
Polk County spreads across the rolling country southwest of Rome, and Elite Catastrophe works it week in and week out. Cedartown, the county seat, and nearby Rockmart both grew up as mill and mining towns, and their older streets still carry the deep canopy that comes with that history: red oaks and water oaks over the in-town blocks, sweetgums along the back fences, and stands of loblolly and shortleaf pine ringing the edges of every neighborhood. Smaller Aragon and the roads between the towns hold the same mix, with pines crowding the margins wherever they have had room to spread.

Anyone who has walked or ridden the Silver Comet Trail knows how much of Polk County's character comes from its trees, and that same canopy is the part of a property most homeowners never think about until something cracks. The clay soil here does trees no favors. It holds water for days after a heavy rain, and a shallow-rooted pine standing in saturated ground is the classic Northwest Georgia blowdown waiting on a gust. Mature hardwoods carry their own risks, dropping large limbs on still afternoons and coming apart at weak unions when the wind finally leans on them.

Our job in Polk County is to keep the trees on your property an asset instead of a liability. That means removing the dead, dying, and badly placed ones before they choose their own schedule, thinning and cleaning the big crowns worth preserving, cutting limbs back off rooflines and service drops, and grinding stumps flush so a removal ends with a lawn rather than an obstacle. When a storm tears through Cedartown, Rockmart, or Aragon, Elite Catastrophe runs crews around the clock until the calls stop coming. The rest of the year, the smartest move a Polk County homeowner can make is to have a questionable tree looked at while every option is still on the table.
gordon-county:
Gordon County wears two faces, and the trees on your property depend on which one your neighborhood belongs to. Calhoun, the county seat, sits right on the I-75 corridor, and it has an established side of in-town streets where water oaks and red oaks have had decades to spread over roofs and power lines, alongside a fast-growing side where new subdivisions keep filling in and the trees are either young plantings or tall pines left over from whatever grew there before. North of Calhoun, Resaca sits quietly on the Oostanaula River amid old battlefield ground, and the smaller communities of Fairmount, Plainville, and Ranger carry the rural canopy of farmhouse hardwoods and roadside pines that Elite Catastrophe knows well.

The problems split along the same lines as the neighborhoods. The old-canopy trouble in Gordon County is the classic kind: big hardwoods holding deadwood over the driveway, water oaks that look full and green while the trunk rots from within, and trees planted a few feet from a foundation back when they were the size of a broomstick. On the newer side, the usual suspect is the leftover loblolly pine, tall and thin and shallow-rooted in red clay, suddenly exposed to wind it never had to stand against while its neighbors surrounded it. After a wet week that clay gives up its grip, and the spring storms do the rest.

We bring the same crew and the same care to Gordon County that we use in our own Rome neighborhoods: removals in tight quarters, precision trimming around roofs and lines, deadwood cleanup in mature crowns, honest hazard evaluations for leaners and storm-scarred trunks, and stump grinding that gives you back a lawn. When severe weather crosses the county, our storm crews respond around the clock. For everything short of that, a calm walk around your property with someone who works on trees every day usually answers the question you have been putting off since the last big blow.
chattooga-county:
Up in Chattooga County the land begins folding toward the Lookout Mountain foothills, and that terrain changes how trees live and how they come down. Summerville, the county seat, and the mill town of Trion both sit among wooded ridges along the Chattooga River, and wind behaves differently here than it does out in flat country. Storms funnel along the valleys, gusts pour over the ridgelines, and the tall pines and slope-side hardwoods around town take the brunt of it. The smaller communities of Lyerly and Menlo carry the same exposed, wooded character. Anyone who has been through a few spring storm seasons here has watched a healthy-looking tree lose half its crown in a single afternoon.

The species list is familiar Northwest Georgia: loblolly and shortleaf pines that snap or uproot in saturated clay, water oaks that hollow with age, red oaks with heavy horizontal limbs, and sweetgums that drop wood in every stiff wind. What the foothills add is exposure. A tree on a slope or a ridge shoulder catches more wind, anchors in thinner soil, and leans harder as it grows toward the light. That lean is often the first thing a homeowner notices, and the last thing they should ignore, because a tree already leaning over a roof has told you exactly what it plans to do in the next storm.

Elite Catastrophe makes the drive up to Chattooga County regularly, because its homeowners live with some of the most storm-tested trees in our whole working area. Our work here leans toward hazard management for that reason: removing dead and declining trees before they come down on their own terms, reducing weight in crowns that have outgrown their root systems, cutting limbs back off rooflines, and grinding stumps so old removals stop being obstacles in the yard. When the weather turns genuinely dangerous, we field storm calls around the clock. The better plan is the boring one, handling the questionable tree this month rather than on the night the radar turns red.
bartow-county:
Bartow County runs from the I-75 corridor east toward Lake Allatoona, and Elite Catastrophe covers all of it. Cartersville, the largest city in the county, and Adairsville to the north both hold every kind of neighborhood, from established streets with heavy mature canopy to newer subdivisions where young landscaping out front hides a wall of leftover pines along the back fence. Emerson sits near Lake Allatoona and the LakePoint complex, Euharlee keeps its historic character around its old covered bridge and creek, and the smaller communities of Kingston, Taylorsville, and White fill in the rural spaces between. It is a county of oaks, sweetgums, and tall loblolly pines, sometimes all on a single lot.

Bartow County trees fail in predictable ways. Mature water oaks decline from the inside, shedding large limbs without warning on calm days and coming apart entirely in storms. Sweetgums drop wood constantly and split at weak unions. The construction-stressed pines are the quiet danger: their roots cut or buried during grading when a subdivision went in, they can look fine for several summers, then brown out, turn brittle, and become the most dangerous tree on the street. The red clay underneath holds water long after a rain, so a saturated spring loosens exactly the trees that are already most exposed.

Our everyday work across Bartow County is the heart of what we do: removing dead, dying, and badly placed trees, trimming limbs off rooflines and away from driveways, thinning and cleaning the big crowns worth preserving, giving honest evaluations of leaners and storm-scarred trunks, and grinding stumps below grade so the yard goes back to being a yard. When a storm system rakes across the county, Elite Catastrophe answers emergency calls around the clock until the last hazard is on the ground. If there is a Bartow County tree you have been watching with one eye all season, the time to get a straight answer about it is while it is still standing.
